findings.title: Role values documented in comment instead of type union
findings.description: The comment enumerates allowed role values ('user' | 'assistant' | 'system') but the field type is plain `string`. If new roles are added or existing ones change, the comment will silently become stale. The allowed values should be enforced by the type system, not by a comment that will rot.
findings.suggestion: Define a string literal union type: `type ConversationRole = 'user' | 'assistant' | 'system'` and use it as the field type instead of documenting the constraint in a comment.

findings.title: Delete method JSDoc calls LanceDB filter a 'SQL filter expression'
findings.code_snippet:
/**
   * Delete vectors matching the SQL filter expression.
   * Example filter: "id = 'v1'" or "source = 'screenshot'"
   */
findings.description: The JSDoc refers to the filter parameter as a 'SQL filter expression.' LanceDB uses its own filter expression syntax which is SQL-like but not actual SQL. Calling it 'SQL filter' could mislead maintainers into thinking full SQL syntax (JOINs, subqueries, etc.) is supported, or that injection risks apply the same way.
findings.suggestion: Change 'SQL filter expression' to 'LanceDB filter expression' or simply 'filter expression' and link to LanceDB documentation for supported syntax.

findings.title: Count method JSDoc restates the method name
findings.description: The JSDoc 'Count the number of rows in the named table' adds no information beyond what the method signature `count(tableName: string): Promise<number>` already communicates. It has negative ROI because it must be maintained alongside the code.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the JSDoc or enhance it with useful context, such as documenting that this counts all rows including soft-deleted ones, or clarifying performance characteristics.

findings.title: Close method comment references 'future compatibility' (rot risk)
findings.description: The comment mentions 'future compatibility' as a justification for the close method's existence. While the explanation of the current LanceDB behavior is valuable, the forward-looking 'future compatibility' claim may rot if LanceDB never adds an explicit close method, leaving a misleading rationale.
findings.suggestion: Keep the factual explanation about LanceDB's behavior but rephrase: 'This method exists for interface symmetry so callers can consistently clean up resources regardless of the underlying storage.' Drop the speculative 'future compatibility' framing.

positive_observations: JSDoc is consistently applied to all exported classes, methods, and interface fields -- good for API discoverability.
The dummy-row creation comment in createTable (line 30) is an excellent 'why' comment explaining a non-obvious LanceDB workaround.
Type documentation in types.ts includes units ('Unix timestamp in milliseconds') and example values ('screenshot', 'conversation', 'document'), which meaningfully reduces ambiguity.
The _distance field documents its semantics ('lower = more similar for L2'), which is critical context that cannot be inferred from the type alone.
No forbidden patterns (TODO, FIXME, HACK, PLACEHOLDER, TEMP) found in any of the files.
